description: A chronic metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance and progressive pancreatic beta cell dysfunction, leading to sustained hyperglycemia and progressive multi-organ complications affecting the cardiovascular, renal, ocular, and nervous systems.

stages.label: Early (0–5 years)
stages.description: Insulin resistance develops; pancreas compensates with hyperinsulinemia; blood glucose begins to rise.

stages.label: Intermediate (5–15 years)
stages.description: Beta cell function declines; sustained hyperglycemia damages microvasculature; early end-organ changes.

stages.label: Advanced (15+ years)
stages.description: Established microvascular and macrovascular complications across multiple organ systems.

cascade.narrationFallback: Pancreatic beta cells become hypersensitive to glucose and begin secreting excess insulin to compensate for rising tissue insulin resistance.

aiContext.clinicalNote: Asymptomatic phase, often missed without targeted screening.

cascade.narrationFallback: Muscle and fat tissues progressively lose sensitivity to insulin, requiring ever-higher insulin levels to maintain normal glucose uptake.

aiContext.clinicalNote: Linked to obesity, sedentary lifestyle, and genetic predisposition.

cascade.narrationFallback: Despite elevated insulin, blood glucose rises above the normal range as compensation begins to fail. This marks the clinical onset of diabetes.
The kidneys initially respond to high blood glucose by increasing filtration rate, a phase known as glomerular hyperfiltration.
Years of overwork drive beta cell exhaustion. Insulin output progressively declines, accelerating the rise in blood glucose.

aiContext.clinicalNote: Many patients now require oral medications or insulin.

cascade.narrationFallback: Sustained high glucose causes thickening of small blood vessel walls throughout the body, reducing oxygen delivery to tissues.

aiContext.physiology: advanced glycation end products and microvascular damage
aiContext.clinicalNote: The mechanism behind the major complications that follow.

cascade.narrationFallback: Damaged retinal capillaries begin to leak and form microaneurysms, the earliest visible sign of diabetic eye disease.

aiContext.clinicalNote: Often asymptomatic until significant; annual eye exams critical.

cascade.narrationFallback: Peripheral nerves, especially in the feet and legs, lose function due to vascular damage. Patients may notice tingling or reduced sensation.

aiContext.clinicalNote: Increases risk of unnoticed foot injuries and ulcers.

cascade.narrationFallback: Years of glomerular damage progress to chronic kidney disease. Kidney function declines, protein leaks into the urine, and waste products accumulate.

aiContext.clinicalNote: Diabetes is the leading cause of end-stage renal disease worldwide.

cascade.narrationFallback: Diabetes accelerates atherosclerosis throughout the body. Coronary arteries narrow, dramatically increasing the risk of heart attack.

aiContext.clinicalNote: Cardiovascular disease causes the majority of deaths in T2DM.

cascade.narrationFallback: Without intervention, fragile new blood vessels grow on the retina and rupture. This is proliferative retinopathy and a leading cause of preventable blindness.

aiContext.clinicalNote: Treatable with laser photocoagulation if caught early.

sources: Swedish National Diabetes Register cohort study (Lancet Diabetes Endocrinol)
